title: alito-voting-rights-fake-data — 2026-05-15
narrative: Justice Samuel Alito's Callais v. US ruling gutted the Voting Rights Act of 1965 — one of the most consequential civil rights laws in American history — citing a Justice Department filing that claimed Black voter turnout exceeded white voter turnout in two of the last five presidential elections. The DOJ used this to argue that Section 2 of the VRA was already "broad enough" to protect voting rights without the preclearance requirements the law originally imposed on states with histories of discrimination. Alito called the data "instructive" in his majority opinion. The data was the empirical backbone of the ruling. The only problem: the data appears to be wrong.
telemetry: Guardian (May 8) — exclusive: the DoJ data cited in Callais v. US has "questionable methodology" per Guardian analysis; the claim does not hold up under scrutiny
Above The Law (May 2026): called the DoJ filing "fake facts in a Supreme Court opinion" — a remarkable characterization from a legal publication
NPR (May 2): the DoJ filing was the sole empirical support for the ruling's key factual premise about minority voter turnout

Census Bureau and Current Population Survey (CPS) data for the relevant elections — 2000, 2004, 2008, 2012, 2016 — shows white voter turnout consistently higher than Black turnout in most cycles, the opposite of what the DoJ filing claimed
The DoJ filing appears to have either inverted the numbers or used a methodology that produced the inverse of reality
This is the first time in modern memory a Supreme Court majority opinion has been built on a verifiable factual error in the government's own filing
analysis: The Voting Rights Act was passed in 1965 after decades of documented voting discrimination against Black Americans — literacy tests, poll taxes, violence. Section 2 prohibited voting practices that discriminated. Section 5 required states with histories of discrimination to get federal approval before changing voting rules — "preclearance." The Callais ruling effectively kills preclearance by claiming Section 2 already covers everything, making Section 5 redundant. The Court accepted the DOJ's factual premise without independent verification. That premise — that Black voter turnout exceeds white voter turnout — is not merely debatable. It appears to be inverted. This means a Supreme Court ruling that will reshape American democracy was decided on the basis of a clerical error or deliberate misrepresentation in a government filing. The Court's own factfinding failed. The DOJ's lawyers who filed the brief either fabricated the numbers, misread the data, or provided data from a source that used a methodology no one has been able to identify. Either way: the opinion stands unless overturned, and the Court has shown no appetite to revisit its own work.
verdict: [SIPHONED VERDICT]: The opinion that killed the Voting Rights Act was built on numbers that don't add up. The DOJ told the Supreme Court that Black voter turnout exceeded white turnout in two of five recent elections. The actual CPS data shows the opposite. Alito called these numbers "instructive" — meaning he built a landmark ruling on a factual claim that is verifiably false. This isn't judicial activism vs. restraint. It's institutional failure: the Court trusted a government filing without checking, and the filing was wrong. If the data had shown what the DOJ claimed, the ruling might still be wrong — but at least it would be wrong for reasons the justices actually considered.
